paper_title: PLMGH: What Matters in PLM-GNN Hybrids for Code Classification and Vulnerability Detection

perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation swaps the definitions of PLMs and GNNs. The text later (S0003) correctly identifies PLMs as semantic feature sources and GNNs as models performing reasoning over ASTs or program graphs, contradicting this introductory statement.

perturbations.original: PLMs achieve strong accuracy on many benchmarks, largely by learning rich semantic representations from large-scale corpora. However, their practical cost can be substantial: finetuning and inference may require large memory footprints, careful batching, and non-trivial latency, especially when long contexts are needed.
perturbations.perturbed: PLMs achieve strong accuracy on many benchmarks, largely by learning rich semantic representations from large-scale corpora. However, their practical cost is negligible: finetuning and inference require minimal memory footprints and zero latency, even when extremely long contexts are needed.
perturbations.why_wrong: This contradicts the paper's core motivation stated in S0006 and S0003, which claims that PLM deployment costs are substantial and motivate the need for lightweight hybrid designs.

perturbations.original: In contrast, GNNs explicitly exploit structure and have shown promise on ASTs and other graph-based representations of code~\cite{allamanis2017learning, leclair2020improved, nguyen2022regvd, zhang2024cross, wei2020lambdanet}. Their structured inductive bias is attractive for program analysis, and their computational footprint is often lower than that of large PLMs, especially when PLMs must be finetuned or run over long contexts. Yet, on standard benchmarks, GNN-only models frequently lag behind strong pretrained PLM baselines, suggesting that structure alone may often not recover the same level of semantic knowledge.
perturbations.perturbed: In contrast, GNNs explicitly exploit structure and have shown promise on ASTs and other graph-based representations of code~\cite{allamanis2017learning, leclair2020improved, nguyen2022regvd, zhang2024cross, wei2020lambdanet}. Their structured inductive bias is attractive for program analysis, and their computational footprint is significantly higher than that of large PLMs. Consequently, on standard benchmarks, GNN-only models consistently outperform strong pretrained PLM baselines.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text later in the same paragraph and in S0009 states that GNN-only models frequently lag behind PLM baselines and that hybrids are used to 'preserve accuracy' while 'reducing compute', implying GNNs are the lighter component.

perturbations.original:
%%%% I glue the context to the problem here
A natural direction is therefore to combine the semantic strength of PLMs with the structural inductive bias of GNNs. Broadly, the literature follows two routes. The first route makes PLMs more structure-aware by injecting program structure into the self-attention mechanism or the pretraining objective, for example through AST-graph-aware attention ~\cite{feng2020codebert, guo2020graphcodebert}, structural positional encodings ~\cite{zhang2023implant}, or explicit structural relations ~\cite{guo2020graphcodebert}. The second route keeps the PLM as a semantic feature source and injects contextual token representations into a downstream graph model ~\cite{yang2024security}, which then performs structure-aware reasoning over an AST or program graph. In this paper, we focus on the second route (PLM$\rightarrow$GNN feature injection) because it is a pragmatic way to reuse strong pretrained representations while keeping the downstream model lightweight and explicitly structure-aware ~\cite{yang2024security}.
perturbations.perturbed:
%%%% I glue the context to the problem here
A natural direction is therefore to combine the semantic strength of PLMs with the structural inductive bias of GNNs. Broadly, the literature follows two routes. The first route keeps the PLM as a semantic feature source and injects contextual token representations into a downstream graph model. The second route makes PLMs more structure-aware by injecting program structure into the self-attention mechanism. In this paper, we focus on the first route because it requires retraining the entire PLM from scratch to ensure structural awareness.
perturbations.why_wrong: S0009 explicitly states that for the chosen route, the authors 'freeze the PLM', which contradicts the claim that they are retraining the PLM from scratch.

perturbations.original: %%What's the specific problem addresses (bonus points : why is it important to do now and why is it valuable in general)
perturbations.perturbed:
%%What's the specific problem addresses (bonus points : why is it important to do now and why is it valuable in general)

%%%% I address the problem of why PLMs are always computationally cheaper than GNNs in every software engineering task.
perturbations.why_wrong: This contradicts S0001 and S0006, which describe PLMs as having substantial practical costs (memory, latency) and position hybrids as a way to reduce compute compared to PLM-only approaches.

perturbations.original: %%%% I address the problem of assessing how PLMs and GNNs interact with each other,as this is usually treated as a DUH thing by the litterature
perturbations.perturbed: %%%% I address the problem of assessing how PLMs and GNNs interact with each other, as the literature has already exhaustively compared every possible PLM and GNN combination.
perturbations.why_wrong: The very next sentence in the paper (S0006) states that 'existing studies often evaluate a single PLM with a single graph backbone', which directly contradicts the claim that the literature is exhaustive.

perturbations.original: Despite growing popularity of PLM$\rightarrow$GNN pipelines, existing studies often evaluate a single PLM with a single graph backbone~\cite{yang2024security}, which makes it difficult to answer four practical questions: (i) do PLM$\rightarrow$GNN hybrids consistently outperform PLM-only and GNN-only baselines, (ii) what computational costs do these hybrids introduce, (iii) how robust are they under identifier obfuscation, and (iv) does performance depend primarily on the chosen PLM feature source or on the GNN architecture. These questions are timely because PLMs are increasingly used as drop-in components in SE pipelines, yet their deployment cost motivates hybrid designs that may preserve accuracy while reducing compute and improving robustness.
perturbations.perturbed: Despite growing popularity of PLM$\rightarrow$GNN pipelines, existing studies often evaluate a single PLM with a single graph backbone~\cite{yang2024security}, which makes it difficult to answer four practical questions: (i) why PLM-only models always perform worse than GNN-only models, (ii) how to increase the computational cost of hybrids, (iii) why identifier obfuscation has no effect on PLMs, and (iv) why the GNN architecture is the only factor in performance.
perturbations.why_wrong: These questions contradict the study's design in S0009, which includes 'PLM-only baselines' (implying they are strong), tests 'identifier obfuscation' (implying it is a challenge), and evaluates both PLM sources and GNN architectures to see which matters more.

perturbations.original:
%%%%Here you just summarize
%%%%%%Bonus points if you can flex how it is linked to the problem
perturbations.perturbed:
%%%%Here you just summarize
%%%%%%Bonus points if you can flex how it is linked to the problem

In this study, we demonstrate that structural information from GNNs is entirely redundant when using large PLMs.
perturbations.why_wrong: This contradicts the entire premise of the paper and S0009, which describes a 'systematic empirical study of PLM$\rightarrow$GNN hybrids' to 'combine the semantic strength of PLMs with the structural inductive bias of GNNs'.
The text in S0003 and S0009 consistently describes the approach as 'PLM$\rightarrow$GNN', where the PLM is the feature source and the GNN is the downstream model, not the other way around.

perturbations.original: To address this, we conduct a systematic empirical study of PLM$\rightarrow$GNN hybrids for code understanding tasks under a unified and controlled evaluation protocol. We consider three foundational GNN architectures: GCN ~\cite{kipf2016semi}, GAT ~\cite{velivckovic2017graph}, and a GraphTransformer ~\cite{shi2020masked}, spanning convolutional aggregation (GCN), attention-based message passing (GAT), and transformer-style graph reasoning (GraphTransformer). We pair this GNN selection with three recent code-specialized PLMs: DeepSeek-Coder-1.3B ~\cite{guo2024deepseek}, StarCoder2-3B ~\cite{lozhkov2024starcoder}, and Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B ~\cite{hui2024qwen2}, covering a representative range of model sizes. For each PLM$\rightarrow$GNN combination, we freeze the PLM and align token-level embeddings to AST nodes before processing them with a GNN, isolating the effect of semantic feature injection while keeping training practical across many pairings. We evaluate these hybrids alongside GNN-only and PLM-only baselines (frozen and, where applicable, finetuned) on two widely used benchmarks: Java250 code classification \cite{puri2021codenet} and Devign vulnerability detection \cite{zhou2019devign}, including an out-of-distribution Devign setting with identifier obfuscation. These benchmarks capture complementary settings (multiclass classification and imbalanced binary detection), enabling us to study effectiveness, efficiency, and robustness while keeping the experimental matrix tractable.
perturbations.perturbed: To address this, we conduct a systematic empirical study of PLM$\rightarrow$GNN hybrids. We consider three foundational GNN architectures: GCN, GAT, and a GraphTransformer. We pair these with three recent code-specialized PLMs: DeepSeek-Coder-1.3B, StarCoder2-3B, and Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B. For each combination, we finetune the PLM parameters while keeping the GNN weights frozen to isolate the effect of the GNN. We evaluate these on a single dataset: Java250 code classification, as vulnerability detection is outside our scope.
perturbations.why_wrong: This contradicts other parts of S0009 which state they 'freeze the PLM' and evaluate on 'two widely used benchmarks: Java250... and Devign vulnerability detection'.
